title: Joy as Practice
subtitle: On joy as something cultivated through attention and small repeated acts, not waited for
excerpt: A reading on the case for treating joy as a practice rather than a state you either have or don't. On why waiting for joy produces less of it, what the research on positive emotion suggests about cultivation, and what small daily practices actually work.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education, drawing on Barbara Fredrickson's broaden-and-build theory of positive emotions (1998 to 2013), Ingrid Fetell Lee's Joyful: The Surprising Power of Ordinary Things to Create Extraordinary Happiness (2018), and the cultivation literature in positive psychology.

content.text: Joy is often treated as something that happens to you, a response to good circumstances, good luck, or the right mood. This framing places it outside your control and makes it reasonable to wait for conditions to improve before expecting it. The research on positive emotion suggests a different model, and the difference has practical consequences.

Joy is a positive emotion with a specific quality. It is expansive. Researchers set it apart from contentment, which is calm and satisfied with no need for more. They also set it apart from excitement, which is high-energy and anticipates something coming. Joy tends to come in moments of sudden beauty, unexpected connection, or a sense of rightness. It opens awareness and possibility for a short time.

It is not the same as happiness in the general sense, and it is not the same as pleasure. Pleasure is the satisfaction of a want. Joy is something more like recognition of something real and good being present.
Why pleasure isn't the same thing

Swapping pleasure in for joy is common and easy to understand. Pleasure is more predictable and quicker to reach. But chasing pleasure on its own, without room for joy, tends to give less back over time. The scroll, the snack, the retail therapy. They satisfy in the moment but do not bring the same opening that joy brings. Barbara Fredrickson's broaden-and-build theory suggests that positive emotions, and joy in particular, build inner resources over time. Pleasure does not build in the same way.

The cultivation approach to joy is simple: you do not wait for the right conditions. You create conditions that make joy more likely, repeatedly and deliberately. This is not the same as forcing joy or performing it. It is about attending to what makes it arise in you specifically, and arranging some amount of regular contact with those things.

What sparks joy is personal and often small. Ingrid Fetell Lee's research on the look and feel of joy found that some physical traits reliably produce a joy response across cultures. Colour, roundness, abundance, and surprise. But the things that produce joy in one person are often simpler than expected. A certain light at a certain time of day, a piece of music, the company of one friend.

Noticing. Spot what produced a moment of joy and log it briefly, so you remember it is there. Tending. Go back on purpose to the small things that bring it on, rather than waiting for them to happen on their own. Protecting. Choose not to fill every space with tasks. Give joy some room to arise.

The most common block to joy is not difficult circumstances. People in difficult circumstances can and do experience joy. The most common block is busyness that leaves no space for it, and the belief that joy must be earned by the completion of prior tasks. The task list is always incomplete. If joy waits on the completion of it, joy will always wait.

Written for homemade.education. The reading draws on Barbara Fredrickson's broaden-and-build theory. It also draws on Ingrid Fetell Lee's book Joyful, and the positive psychology cultivation literature. See the Sources block for the years and editions.
